Off Calendar Oct-10-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's Motion To Seal Documents Filed In Support Of Motion For Summary Judgment, Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication.

Defendant City and County of San Francisco's unopposed Motion To Seal Documents Filed In Support Of Motion For Summary Judgment, Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication is GRANTED.

Preliminarily, Defendant's election to notice the sealing motion for the same day the summary judgment motion is to be heard is unwise. It should be obvious that the sealing motion needs to be resolved long before the hearing on the summary judgment motion (what happens if the court denies the sealing motion?). Summary judgment must be noticed three months out. At the very least, Defendant should have noticed this motion for the first available day after it filed the motion, which, at the latest would be the date they filed their summary judgment moving papers. Its failure to do so is inconsiderate of the impact of any ruling on other parties (what information can or can't they discuss in public filings?) and increases the costs of and adds unnecessary burdens to adjudication of both the motion to seal and the summary judgment motion (at the very least it would likely require a continuous of the summary judgment motion, consuming time and resources), which may already be set precariously close to the trial date (as is true here). Defendant is advised to be more thoughtful in the future. (Additionally, the court is not sure it received courtesy copies of complete sets of the redacted and unredacted filings, as required.)

A court may order that a record be filed under seal only if it expressly finds facts that establish: (1) an overriding interest exists that overcomes the right of public access to the record; (2) the overriding interest supports sealing the records; (3) a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; (4) the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and, (5) no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. (Cal. Rules of Ct., rule 2.550(d)(1)-(5); NBC Subsidiary (KNBC-TV), Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (Locke) (1999) 20 Cal.4th 1178, 1216-1218 ("NBC Subsidiary").) The party seeking the sealing of a record bears the burden of presenting information sufficient to identify the specific information claimed to be entitled to such treatment and the nature of the harm threatened by disclosure. (H.B. Fuller Co. v. Doe (2007) 151 Cal.App.4th 879, 894.) The moving party must set forth specific facts in support of a motion to seal; statements that are too conclusory, abstract, or generic are not sufficient to satisfy the burden. (Id. at pp. 895-896.)

Good case appearing, the motion to seal is granted. The redacted versions of the relevant pleadings shall remain in the public record.

Defendant is ordered to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the above text, adding text to comply with California Rules of Court, rule 2.550(d), and email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the time set for hearing.

The proposed order submitted prior to the hearing did not include the findings per the instruction of the court and is not signed. Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the court's ruling which include findings and must drop a hard courtesy copy in Dept. 302's inbox.
